content:
Predictions about where the industry is heading

12 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

For over fifteen years my job has been building, open sourcing, evangelizing and selling productivity tools to software developers. It wasn’t a conscious career choice. More like one thing led to another, and it was fun, so that’s what I did.
Now I’m pondering what this work looks like post-LLMs. It’s on my mind a lot because, as always, I’m currently working on developer tools.
This is a thinking-through-writing essay and covers some personal predictions about what the next five years of tooling progress might look like. It doesn’t represent the views of my anonymous employer, which as far as I know doesn’t have any position on the matter.

T+0 days: Bewilderment
The most common reaction when I say I’m working on a new programming language is surprise. Why bother? It’s not like anyone is going to use it. If you believe that nearly all code in future will be written by LLMs (and I do) then it’s reasonable to ask:

• How will they learn a new tool without examples in the training set?

• Who cares what tools models use anyway? It’s not like they’re going to complain, or go work for a competitor because your tech stack is obsolete.
But I think there are good answers to these questions.
Let’s start with the training set. Is there really a catch-22 here? No, for two reasons. The first is obvious enough: LLMs support in-context learning where you load skills into the context window. This increases costs because the skill has to be processed on every single server round-trip (cached tokens are only cheap, not free). But there’s clearly a break even point here: for some skills the token savings are big enough to pay for themselves.
The second reason is a bit less obvious. Modern LLM training exposes the model to trillions of tokens, but it’s common that each token in the training set is only seen once. A conventional understanding of ML says you need millions of examples to learn a new skill, but this isn’t true when thinking about LLMs learning developer tools. Frontier models can remember the details of very obscure tools that have almost no examples in the training set. For example try asking about Hydraulic Conveyor, a tool for deploying desktop apps. I own the company that sells it, but although it’s free for open source apps it’s mostly used by proprietary software. So there’s only a handful of config file examples on GitHub — probably fewer than 50. Yet the frontier models know all about it and can use it fluently.
I suspect the frontier labs are all using curriculum learning, so by the time they’re introduced to the documentation for these tools they’ve already mastered all the knowledge needed to understand them and the details can be successfully encoded into the weights with a single backwards pass.
In other words, getting knowledge into models is actually quite easy. You don’t need a massive community creating millions of examples. As long as the crawlers find your website and as long as that website has plenty of docs, the models will get some understanding of how to use it. They might not memorize every single detail but they’ll remember enough to be useful. The expensive period in which you need to explain everything in a skill is not necessarily long. Soon enough, all you need for LLM success is a tiny skill that only contains the changes from what it learned to the current version.
Now, an LLM won’t organically choose your obscure new tool if all you have is a website. The model has ‘opinions’ about what it ‘wants’ to use, and affecting those is harder. We’ll come back to that later.
The second reasonable question people ask is, “Does it even matter what tools models use?” Companies equip humans with better tools hoping for cost savings, lower time-to-market, improved morale, and maybe an advantage in the hiring market. Humans like adopting new tools because they’re more enjoyable or productive to use, to buff their CV or because they’re bored. But language models are cheap, don’t suffer morale problems, don’t write CVs and you can hire them with just a credit card.
Yet the cost argument remains. LLMs and humans differ in many ways but better tools make both of them work faster and therefore cheaper. Fast and cheap is good. That’s the only motive you need to continue creating new tools, languages and abstractions.

T+1 year: Proof of Productivity
I think by mid 2027 these sorts of conversations will have stopped happening and we’ll have new conventional wisdom: developer tools have value because they reduce token costs and wall clock time. Although hardly anyone is advertising tools this way today, soon enough everyone will think it was obvious from the start.
Some big changes are implied by this goal. One is that development of any kind of tool comes to look like working on a compiler.
Historically most developer tools were adopted based on intuition, gut feeling, likeability of the creators or community momentum. Barely disguised emotions were often decisive. Nowhere was this more true than in programming languages and JavaScript frameworks, which inspired decades of relentless intellectual warring.
It made sense for people to take this stuff seriously — learning a new tech represented a large investment of time and energy. Bet on the wrong horse and it could mean permanent damage to your career. And you can’t do randomized controlled experiments on developers, so there was no way for anyone to unambiguously win these debates.
But doing controlled experiments on LLMs is possible — even easy.
So I think by this time next year we’ll see new GitHub projects come with a set of metrics giving the token efficiency improvement over some competing approach, plus/minus a confidence interval, in the same way that model companies show metrics changes today. Probably some projects will even compare the token efficiency of old versions versus new as a way to advertise the upgrades, like compilers do. And there’ll be some YC backed startup offering to generate these comparisons as a service … but most projects will just use CI jobs.
Commercial tooling developers like the big clouds, database companies and so on will probably start doing weekly trials, nightly trials or even trials on every merge to quickly locate drops in token efficiency. Bugs will be filed demanding rollbacks with titles like, “Token usage regressed by 5%” or “wall clock time increased 12%”.

T+2 years: Code is hard, let’s go shopping!
With controlled experiments set up you can prove that a tool is worth money … and more importantly, how much. This could mean big changes to the shape of the software industry.
Developer tools aren’t a great market. Developers really hate paying for tools. We’d usually rather use something open source and terrible than something commercial but good.
There are rational reasons for this. We fear lockin, price hikes and vendors exiting the market. Rewrites are so expensive and disruptive that we seek the safety of liberally licensed components. And the convenience of adopting an open source tool just can’t be beat.
Yet as coders shift to become managers of token budgets this way of thinking will experience big challenges. Can you really justify to your boss using an open source tool when there’s hard data proving that a commercial equivalent would save money? Maybe not, and for some this will be a culture shock. I expect drama.
In the 1990s the rise of OOP led to many heady predictions of online marketplaces, in which devs would casually purchase ActiveX controls or Delphi components. Although a handful of businesses did briefly make money this way, the vision never panned out because it was obsoleted by Perl’s CPAN model — vast centralized repositories of open source libraries. Developers enthusiastically signed up to populate these repositories, motivated by a mixture of (at first) Stallmanist ideology and (later) fame, jobs or open-core business models.
This will be controversial but my sense is that open source development is slowly running out of energy. Stallmanist ideology died years ago; new projects almost never use copyleft licenses and in fact FreeBSD just announced the removal of the last bit of copyleft code from their OS. Having GitHub projects on your résumé was once a differentiator, but now everyone has them. And people are more aware these days that creating a popular open source library might make you this guy:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

xkcd: DependencyI’ve created open source projects before. I’ll probably do so again. But I probably won’t do so as an unpaid volunteer. Nobody wants employees of random tech giants demanding security fixes from you at 3am on a Sunday, decades after your project stopped being cool.
The move to LLM driven coding looks to me like the final nail in the coffin for voluntary open source development. The expected outcome of creating a cool open source library is now that an LLM finds it while doing a web search the developer never sees, adopts it, codes with it and maybe even files a bug report — without ever saying thank you, giving you a job opportunity or contributing anything back. You optimized some matrix multiplications and in return got nothing except people who expect you to keep doing so. What’s the point?

Get Mike Hearn’s stories in your inbox

Remember me for faster sign in

So although commercial component marketplaces didn’t really happen in the 90s, this time I think they just might. A new generation of startups will arise, pitching themselves as “Steam for Developers”.
The right model for such stores is an open question. The industry figured out formulas that work for consumer software: reviews, payment processing, discovery that works and more, all in return for a revenue share. What works for developers is less clear.
In DevSteam we might see the following:

• A usage based model in which the store tracks your coding agent logs to figure out how many tokens are spent interacting with the tools. The cost you’re charged scales with that, i.e. not based on your total revenue but rather how much you ‘interact’ with the tool (or library, etc). Price guides showing expected costs for common tasks would make it more concrete.

• The stores would act as trusted intermediaries that run the benchmarks for the products they sell, meaning you have some sort of independent assurance that buying a product really does save money over the alternative.

• The stores will come harness plugins that search the stores in the background looking for tools relevant to the current task. If the store realizes it has a tool that could accelerate the agent’s current task, it would ‘advertise’ to the model, which might choose to buy it out of its pocket money. And users would want this because the expected outcome is that the task finishes faster and cheaper, even with the cost of the new tool added on top.

• Source code might be available, but with licenses that forbid redistribution.
Because a part of the store is a discovery engine for the model, the question of what a model ‘wants’ to use (based on popularity) becomes much less important. It has hard data that can be used to make a scientific decision instead.
They might also try to solve the sandboxing problem. Once you become a trusted intermediary between developers and their tools, the sky is the limit.

T+3 years: The age of insurance
Open source libraries are attractive not just due to their rock-bottom price and frictionless adoption process, but also because they hedge supply chain risk. Projects might be abandoned by their maintainers, but if necessary you can take over and make whatever changes are needed. In the limit you can fork the project and become the new maintainers, assembling a coalition of other users to spread the costs.
